sentences.text: So I escaped.
And at night, I-- I was by the farmers, and I didn't want them to know me.
So at night, I slept in the barns with-- one night with the horses, with the cows, with the pigs.
And when it got light, around 5 o'clock in the morning or 6 o'clock, before the-- excuse me-- before the farmers got up, I ran away from the barns.
And I went back to the forest.
I think it was in September.
It was already even cold-- very cold and raining.
Because it was the slaughter house.
It was yahrzeit.
It's before the Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur.
So usually September, I-- you know, because I don't-- I do yahrzeit.
I-- and I lit some candles.
Because I know this and this-- 12 days in [NON-ENGLISH].
So what-- what month is this?
You don't know?
Anyways, so I was-- I was sitting in the forest in there, on the trees.
And I was eating.
And then it started to be very cold and raining.
And I am just bare feet in nothing.
I was almost starving from hunger.
So I made up my mind that I'm gonna go down.
And I didn't know where I-- what-- what-- because I ran away afterwards in a different, you know, village.
And I made up my mind if I'm gonna see a road, and I'm gonna see the people, whatever is gonna go.
So I had to say just one word.
I'm a Jewish girl.
And that said-- in that time, they were digging for the Jewish people.
And they had, at that time, was on a-- on salt-- was a shortage on salt.
So if you kill a Jew, no matter what Jew, a boy, a little girl, whatever, you get a pint-- a reward-- a pint of salt.
So they were-- so the Polish people, the Ukrainian people, they were trying.
They were helping to find Jewish people.
And they would get a pint of salt, because it was an epidemic.
It was very shortage in salt.
So I made up my mind that this is the road, and I'm gonna say I'm a Jewish girl.
And that's all I have to say.
And they'll gonna kill me.
And that's gonna end of my life.
So while I met two boys-- and I got shivering.
I got very, you know a shiver over me.
And I got scared.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
[CRYING]
I met two men, and I wanted to say that I'm a Jewish girl.
And that's all.
They're gonna kill me.
So the minute I met them, I got scared.
And I was not afraid as much as they're gonna kill me, but I was afraid they're gonna rape me again.
And they're gonna take advantage at me.
And I got scared.
And I made up a story-- like an angel was behind my back-- and said that I have a step-- I have a father.
He is an alcoholic.
And I have a stepmother, and she's beating me up.
And I had to run away from the house.
I had a miserable life.
And I'm going [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], that means I wanted to be a maid-- to work in the houses.
So-- and I said the-- the name-- what I-- and I said the town, from one town to another, far away, that they shouldn't be able to find.
And because there's nothing true.
I just made up the story.
And they let me go.
So when they let me go, I walked in in a house on a farm.
I walked in in the house.
And then I said-- I told them the same story.
And they told me if I went to church.
I said, yes.
If I know how to, you know, to say the [NON-ENGLISH].
I said, yes.
And he said, OK.
You go ahead and work for us.
Fine.
I was working.
I was milking the cows.
I was giving the pigs food and everything.
And then what I made the mistake is it was their holiday, and I didn't know the gentile's holiday, what kind of holiday is this.
So they went to church.
And I thought to myself, I'm gonna to show them how nice and clean and neat I am.
And I'm gonna clean up the house-- I'll make spotless.
And they'll gonna come home, and they're gonna love it.
And they came in.
The house was spotless.
And I noticed right away on his face, the owner from the house, that he had a long face.
And he doesn't talk to me like he usually-- the right way.
So he went to the police, and he said that he has a Jewish girl at his house, and maybe he'll get a pint of salt, a reward.
I didn't offer him anything.
He didn't say anything at home.
They came in around midnight, 12 o'clock midnight, and they say to me-- get-- they're not asking any questions or anything-- get dressed.
So I figured I'm in their hands-- with the rifles.
And I got dressed.
And they taking me to the police station.
And that was a few miles, maybe a mile away.
So then while they're taking me-- and on the road one says to the other, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means the heck with her.
Why should we take her to the police?
Let's kill her now.
And we'll go home to our wives, to our family, to sleep.
And they decided to kill me.
So I stood.
They told me like this.
Wait, wait.
And I stood like that-- and the hands over here.
And I closed my eyes, and I was shivering.
And I here already like that.
And I was shivering.
And I hear already the click-- you know, that already to pull the trigger.
And I say it's like an angel was talking behind my back.
And I say [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, wait awhile.
So I say to him, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, you are not killing a Jewish girl.
You killing your own flesh and blood.
I'm a Ukrainian girl, just like you.
I say, no matter what, my father is an alcoholic.
I have a stepmother.
But still I'm his daughter, and he's gonna look for me.
And he's gonna find.
And I say, do you have a family?
Do you have children?
To both of them I start to speak.
And they say, yes.
I say you won't to be able to sleep at night.
You gonna have on your conscious that you killing a [NON-ENGLISH] and that-- not a Jewish girl.
And you'll have on your conscious.
And one looks at the other and say, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, the heck with her.
Let her go.
And he pulled-- pulled down the-- the-- the rifle, and they let me go.
When they let me go-- so I didn't go back to that house again.
Because I thought to myself he's the one who went to the police station and told that I'm a Jewish girl.
And--
They didn't know.
I was acting as a [NON-ENGLISH].
They didn't know.
Because I didn't look-- I don't resemble like a Jewish girl.
And I spoke fluently [NON-ENGLISH]--
--fluently Polish, just like-- just like Jewish, my native language.
And-- and I worked very hard.
And the Jewish kid, they said they don't know.
I'm not-- you're not Jewish.
Like for instance, I went on the field, and I was digging already potatoes.
And the men comes in.
And he brings-- they were looking that time for the police.
And they come in, and they watching me how I'm working.
And they staying and watching-- two policemen.
And they say to the owner, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means she's not Jewish-- that a Jewish girl can't work as hard on the field.
Never in life will a Jewish girl work as hard as I worked.
And they left me, and they went away from the field.
Because they went to church.
And I cleaned the house on the day from the holidays.
And I had no business-- I should've cleaned a day before.
And I cleaned that during the day.
I thought to myself, I'll show him how brave I am, and how good I am, and how clean I am.
And they'll be happy.
And it was vice versa, because in-- on the holidays, the days they go to church to pray and everything, and here I'm cleaning the house.
So that's why he went to the police and told them that I'm a Jewish-- that I'm not a [NON-ENGLISH].
I'm a Jewish girl.
No.
Never.
No.
When I was-- then after with a week-- so I didn't go to their house no more.
I figured, if he went and he said I'm a-- I'm a Jewish girl, I might do something else something wrong.
Because I didn't know the Ukrainian, you know, the-- the holidays and everything.
So I didn't go there.
So it was like the hay-- the stacks of hay, you know, wintertime for the cows.
So I slept.
I crawled in the stack of the hay, and I slept over the night.
And I-- the only thing I asked them what kind of village is that.
And they told me like it's [NON-ENGLISH].
That's not far from [NON-ENGLISH], another city, which is, let's say, like Oak Park, Southfield.
You know.
Very close in proximity.
Very close-- one to the other.
So I was over there.
And there was already more Ukrainian people than Polish people.
So I got up in the morning, and I went for then.
And I came in, and I said the same thing, that I'm looking for work.
And he says to me if you would have some kind of a metric, some kind of papers to-- to-- to prove.
I said, no, I don't have.
So he didn't.
He was afraid to take me, because the Germans, they would have killed them too.
And they would have burned their house.
So I walked away.
And I came in another house.
It was in the afternoon.
And the Jewish kids came from school.
So-- and they went to another room.
So what I did, I took a piece of paper, and I took a pencil, I stealed it.
And I put it in my bosom.
And I walked away.
And I went again under a stack of hay.
I walked for awhile.
And I sat, and I wrote, I'm Hannah.
My name became Hannah [NON-ENGLISH].
Hannah, that's a Ukrainian name.
Hannah-- you see?
I still remember up til now-- Hannah [NON-ENGLISH].
And I'm looking for work.
And I wrote that my father is an alcoholic.
My stepmother is very mean to me.
And I signed the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
That means the mayor and the-- the worker under the mayor.
I signed with my right hand.
So I walked in and I gave them the-- and he-- they're asking me for a piece of paper, if I have something to prove.
And I show it to them.
And he says to me, how-- something is fishing, because you signed with one signature the mayor and the-- how do you call like a-- a president, vice president?
The lower the lower count?
Like the governor or the--
Something like that.
So he says it sounds-- and there's no stamp.
You don't have any stamps.
So I said, you know what?
Now is the war, and he's very busy.
And they're busy with the-- with the Germans, to look for the Jew, to kill the Jew, and everything.
And I came in this moment.
And the-- the police was bothering him.
So he said he didn't want me I should bother him too much.
So he said [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, the heck with you.
And he signed his name, and he signed the other-- this name.
And a stamp, I say, he was looking and looking, and he couldn't find a stamp.
So I gave him this answer, and he took it for granted.
And he said, OK, you can work at me.
While I was working there-- so then it became two undergrounds organizations, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], that means Ukrainian underground.
And [NON-ENGLISH], it's Polish underground.
And one start to fight against the other.
The Poles start to fight against the Ukrainian.
When they start to fight, so they became more softer towards the Jewish people.
So while I was there, and they became already, you know, like enemies against the other and everything.
And then one day, a Jewish man walks in into this house.
And he walks into this house, and he says-- he became there-- the man, he became very friendly and everything.
And I was standing there.
And he says-- he says to him, do you-- do you see her?
She's the same like you are.
That means he's a Jew.
I'm a Jewish girl too.
Because when he became very friendly, and the Jewish people came out, then I saw myself that I'm not the only one that I survived.
There's a lot more Jewish people survived, and they were in the bunkers in the ground, in the forest, in the ground.
They digged out real deep, deep holes.
And on-- on a side, they put lumber.
And on the top of the lumber, they put, you know, from the trees-- branches-- not to recognize.
And they lived in the ground.
And at night, they used to go to the field and dig potatoes, steal potatoes and everything.
And they used to cook.
So they find out there is-- in the forest, there's some more Jewish people.
Because they became already more friendlier, because they are afraid-- one organization against the other.
So-- and so they became friendlier to the Jewish people.
And I saw already that quite a few survived.
I'm not the only one.
So they knew.
One day, the German people came in this town to-- to-- to hunt for-- for the Jew.
So they came with sticks, and they came with-- with dogs.
So two boys came into this house, and they say [NON-ENGLISH].
That means goddammit, you Jewish girl.
[NON-ENGLISH].
That means we hiding you.
They knew already that I'm Jewish.
We hiding you, you have to-- you have to live with us.
You have to-- give us some sex for us, because we hiding you from the German.
Otherwise, we going right away to the German.
We gonna tell them that you are this, and they gonna kill you.
And I said to them, you're not gonna get sex from me, because I don't care.
You can kill me right now.
I have nothing to lose.
I lost my family and everything.
I'm the only one survived.
I don't know how long I'm gonna be alive.
And I'm not gonna give in to you.
I'm sorry.
And I say, I'm still a virgin, and I'm not gonna give in to you.
So they were standing.
Over there was a pail that I prepared potatoes with water for the cows, for the pigs to eat, to take outside.
And I was like from the table up to the window.
And I stood.
So they gave him a kick over here in this bone, in the back with their legs.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And they gave him a kick.
So I jumped from there all the way in-- there was a big round pail with potatoes and water like for the-- for the-- real loose-- not to take.
And I fell into this pail all the way.
So I couldn't walk, like quite a few days.
It was like kind of broken.
It was so painful.
I-- I had to-- bend down.
OK.
OK.
We're gonna pause here.
This is the end of tape three in an interview with Edith Cimmer.
